title: Anatomy of a wealth creator
intro: Four minutes on how an ordinary-looking small company becomes a decades-long compounder.

steps.title: It looks unremarkable at the start
steps.body:
In FY01, **Eicher Motors** was worth ₹46 crore, **Marico** ₹41 crore, **Titan** ₹176 crore. Today they're giants.

The lesson isn't 'buy small caps' — it's that the eventual winners looked ordinary, and often *expensive*, precisely while they were building the thing that would make them great.

steps.title: The build phase hides the moat
steps.body:
First a company spends to **build** — distribution, a brand, a process, a platform. Return on capital can *dip*. It looks dull and costly, and impatient investors leave here.

> [!warn] The most fragile, unexciting phase is often right before the moat forms. Leaving on the dip is leaving at the worst moment.

steps.title: Earnings are the only growth that counts
steps.body:
When the moat holds — pricing power, switching costs, brand trust — **return on capital rises and earnings compound**. Over years, returns track earnings almost exactly.

Watch a small base compound. Drag the knobs:

steps.title: Capital allocation + runway decide the rest
steps.body:
The finishers **reinvest** into deepening the advantage rather than paying out early or diversifying into unrelated bets — and they operate in a **large market still being organised** (the runway).

Danger changes with size: small caps risk governance and leverage; large caps risk **complacency** as moats stop widening.

recap:
**A wealth creator compounds earnings, it doesn't just have a story.**

- Build phase → ROCE dips → moat forms → ROCE rises → earnings compound.
- Returns track **earnings**, not narratives.
- Winners **reinvest** into the moat and have a long **runway**.

Track earnings and return on capital, not the price chart.
